sourceMovie.title: Stand by Me
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Audiences latched onto Stand by Me (1986) as a crime theatrical experience: big enough for a group watch, specific enough to reward a second viewing once you know where it's headed. The crime and drama blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Jackie Brown
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The crime energy of Stand by Me is alive in Jackie Brown (1997), filtered through a different story but the same emotional register. It earns its place on this list with a dark payoff that rivals the original pick.

recommendations.title: The Devil All the Time
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Devil All the Time (2020) operates in the same crime as Stand by Me, but with its own distinct voice and pacing. The dark payoff will feel satisfyingly familiar without retreading the same ground.

recommendations.title: The Outsiders
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Outsiders (1983) belongs to the same conversation as Stand by Me — both anchor their crime in drama emotion that doesn't let go. The tense undercurrent connects them more than any plot summary would suggest.

recommendations.title: Oliver Twist
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Stand by Me's crime stayed with you, Oliver Twist (2005) delivers the same sensation through an entirely different story. The drama through-line is the real common thread between them.

recommendations.title: Bad Education
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Where Stand by Me excels in crime, Bad Education (2004) doubles down on drama to comparable effect. The tonal similarities run deep enough that watching both back-to-back feels intentional.

recommendations.title: At Close Range
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: At Close Range (1986) is the natural next step after Stand by Me: same crime DNA, different perspective. The dark streak and drama elevate it well above a simple genre exercise.

recommendations.title: Oliver Twist
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Fans of Stand by Me who respond to its crime will find Oliver Twist (1948) immediately familiar. Both films share a drama sensibility without ever feeling like carbon copies of each other.

recommendations.title: It
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: There's a reason Stand by Me fans keep landing on It (2017) — the drama is unmistakably related even when the plots diverge. Both films operate on the same emotional frequency, especially in their third acts.

recommendations.title: Dead Poets Society
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Dead Poets Society draws from the same drama well as Stand by Me, channeling atmosphere in ways that feel unmistakably related. The 1989 film makes a natural companion watch — similar in spirit but distinctly its own.

recommendations.title: Apocalypse Now
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Apocalypse Now (1979) captures the drama appeal of Stand by Me without simply imitating it. The war streak running through both films is what puts Apocalypse Now at the top of any "if you liked…" shortlist.

faq.question: What should I watch after Stand by Me?
faq.answer: Try Jackie Brown, The Devil All the Time, The Outsiders—each shares DNA with Stand by Me while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Stand by Me?
faq.answer: Stand by Me (1986) is best described as Crime, Drama. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Stand by Me worth revisiting?
faq.answer: Viewer scores sit around 7.8/10 on TMDB—high enough that replays are common for people who like this crime lane.
